text:
The two-stage time series design is a cornerstone of environmental epidemiology. In the last decade, distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) have become widely used as they allow to model at the same time non-linearity and lag effects. Meanwhile, multivariate meta-analysis (MVM) has been introduced as a sophisticated tool to combine multi-parameter associations. However, there remains a need for better summarising multi-location analyses, as the MVM of DLNM estimates is statistically and computationally challenged due to the large number of parameters involved.

The previously mentioned methodological developments aim at closing this gap. In this paper, we develop a new methodology that allows to translate high-dimensional DLNM into simpler one dimensional summaries, for example, the overall cumulative exposure–response relation or the lag specific exposure–response relation. These can be formulated through a reduced set of parameters that are compatible with multivariate meta-analysis while keeping the full flexibility of the original models.

Using this method, we illustrate a 10-region case study of temperature-related mortality in England and Wales. The advantages of the method are illustrated in the accompanying R code using the dlnm and mvmeta packages. Our application of the method brings together detailed, non-linear, temporally varied estimates of the association between temperature and mortality at the level of individual urban populations, in a manner that circumvents assumptions and simplifications, such as the use of simple moving averages, that are generally adopted in multi-city analyses.
explanation: The passage uses precise domain-specific terminology and a plausible structure for an epidemiology abstract, suggesting human authorship. However, advanced AI could also generate equally plausible text, so the probability is modest.
